meta.title: Getting Started
meta.description: What this course is, who it's for, and why React literacy matters in the age of AI.

blocks.content:
## You are not here to become a React developer

This course will not teach you to build React applications from scratch. It will not ask you to set up a development environment, configure webpack, or write components in a blank editor.

So what *will* it do?

It will teach you to **read** React code. To look at a component and understand what it renders, how data flows through it, and why it might be broken — the same way a project manager can read an architectural blueprint without being an architect.

## Why React, and why now?

React is the most widely used framework for building web applications. When you ask AI to build you a web app, dashboard, or interactive tool, there is a very good chance the output will be React code.

AI can generate entire React applications from a text description. But here is the problem: **AI makes the same React mistakes over and over.** Missing dependency arrays that cause infinite loops. Stale closures that show old data. Unnecessary state that adds complexity for no reason.

Someone needs to:

- Look at a React component and understand what it does
- Spot when a hook is misconfigured
- Recognize why a list is rendering incorrectly
- Describe the problem precisely enough for AI to fix it

That someone is you.

## Prerequisites

This course assumes you are comfortable reading basic HTML, CSS, and JavaScript — the material covered in **Web Fundamentals Levels 1-4**. If you completed that course, you are ready. If you already know the basics of reading web code, you can jump straight in.

You do **not** need to know React. That is what this course teaches.

## What you will learn

| Level | Topic | What you will be able to do |
| :-- | :-- | :-- |
| 1 | **The Component** | Read JSX and understand what a React component renders |
| 2 | **The Data Flow** | Trace how data moves through props from parent to child components |
| 3 | **The Memory** | Read hooks (useState, useEffect, useContext) and understand state changes |
| 4 | **The Side Effects** | Understand data fetching, forms, routing, and async patterns |
| 5 | **The Shape of Code** | Read TypeScript annotations and recognize common project patterns |
| 6 | **The Debugging** | Use error messages and React DevTools to find and diagnose bugs |
| 7 | **The AI Review** | Review AI-generated React code systematically and direct fixes |
| 8 | **The Codebase** | Navigate a real React project and trace features across files |

## How this course works

Each module follows a pattern:

1. **Predict** — You guess before you learn, activating what you already know
2. **Learn** — Short, focused explanations with real React code examples
3. **Practice** — Problems that present real code and ask: "What does this do?" "What is wrong?" "What would you tell AI to fix?"
4. **Reflect** — Connect what you learned to your own work

The practice problems are not trivia. They are the exact situations you will face when reviewing AI-generated React code.

## Who this is for

- **Product managers** who work with React teams and want to understand technical discussions
- **Designers** who want to verify that a developer (or AI) implemented their design correctly in React
- **Founders** who use AI tools to build React applications and need to review the output
- **Non-React developers** who encounter React code in their work and want to read it confidently
- **Anyone** who completed Web Fundamentals and wants to go deeper into the framework that powers most modern web apps

React literacy is not about writing React code — it is about reading, understanding, and troubleshooting it. In the age of AI, the ability to review and debug React components is more valuable than the ability to build them from scratch.

blocks.prompt: Ready to start? Level 1 begins with the building block of every React application: the component. You already know HTML — JSX is going to feel surprisingly familiar.
